title: The number painter of Marrakech
summary: In Marrakech, a man paints house numbers by hand, and explains why a crooked number is sometimes the right one.
body:
In the old part of Marrakech, the streets are narrow. They turn left and right with no plan. The houses do not have street signs like in new cities. Instead, a man named Hamid paints the numbers on the doors by hand. He has done this since he was a boy. His father did it before him.

Hamid does not use a ruler. He looks at the door, he dips his small brush in blue paint, and he writes the number. Sometimes the number is a little crooked. A tourist once said to him, 'Your seven is not straight.' Hamid laughed. He said, 'The door is old. It leans to the left. If I paint a straight seven on a leaning door, the seven looks wrong. A crooked seven on this door looks right.'

The city wanted to change this. They printed numbers on metal plates, all the same, all straight. They put the plates on a few doors. But the people did not like them. An old woman said, 'I knew my door by Hamid's blue four. Now my door looks like every other door. I walked past my own home.'

So the city stopped. Hamid still walks the narrow streets with his brush and his small can of blue. He knows that one day his hand will shake too much, and a machine will print the numbers. But for now, every door in his quarter wears a number that no other door in the world has. The postman knows them. The children know them. And nobody walks past their own home.

annotations.meaningVi: nhúng chiếc cọ nhỏ của ông

annotations.phrase: I walked past my own home
annotations.meaningVi: tôi đi ngang qua chính nhà mình

grammarNotes.title: Conditional 'if' for a general truth
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng 'if + hiện tại đơn, hiện tại đơn' để nói một điều luôn đúng, hoặc dùng kết quả với 'looks/is'. Trong bài: 'If I paint a straight seven on a leaning door, the seven looks wrong.' Cấu trúc: 'If + chủ ngữ + động từ hiện tại, chủ ngữ + động từ hiện tại.' Dùng khi nói nguyên nhân–kết quả chung, không nói thời điểm cụ thể.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 uses 'if + present simple, present simple' for a general truth or rule. From the passage: 'If I paint a straight seven on a leaning door, the seven looks wrong.' Structure: 'If + subject + present verb, subject + present verb.' Use it for a general cause-and-effect that is always true, not a one-time future event.

translationVi:
Ở khu phố cổ Marrakech, các con đường rất hẹp. Chúng rẽ trái rẽ phải không theo quy hoạch nào. Những ngôi nhà không có biển tên đường như ở các thành phố mới. Thay vào đó, một người tên Hamid vẽ các con số lên cánh cửa bằng tay. Ông làm việc này từ khi còn là cậu bé. Cha ông đã làm trước ông.

Hamid không dùng thước kẻ. Ông nhìn cánh cửa, nhúng chiếc cọ nhỏ vào sơn xanh, rồi viết con số. Đôi khi con số hơi xiêu. Một du khách từng nói với ông: 'Số bảy của ông không thẳng.' Hamid cười. Ông nói: 'Cánh cửa đã cũ. Nó nghiêng sang trái. Nếu tôi vẽ một số bảy thẳng trên một cánh cửa nghiêng, số bảy trông sai. Một số bảy hơi xiêu trên cánh cửa này trông lại đúng.'

Thành phố muốn thay đổi điều này. Họ in các con số lên những tấm kim loại, tất cả giống nhau, tất cả đều thẳng. Họ gắn các tấm biển lên vài cánh cửa. Nhưng người dân không thích chúng. Một bà cụ nói: 'Tôi nhận ra cửa nhà mình nhờ số bốn màu xanh của Hamid. Giờ cửa nhà tôi trông giống mọi cánh cửa khác. Tôi đã đi ngang qua chính nhà mình.'

Vì vậy thành phố dừng lại. Hamid vẫn đi khắp những con phố hẹp với chiếc cọ và lon sơn xanh nhỏ. Ông biết một ngày nào đó tay ông sẽ run quá nhiều, và một cái máy sẽ in các con số. Nhưng bây giờ, mỗi cánh cửa trong khu phố của ông mang một con số mà không cánh cửa nào khác trên thế giới có. Người đưa thư biết chúng. Lũ trẻ biết chúng. Và không ai đi ngang qua chính nhà mình.

questions.prompt: Why are house numbers painted by hand in old Marrakech?
options: The narrow streets have no street signs like new cities.
questions.explanation: 'The houses do not have street signs... Instead, a man... paints the numbers.'

questions.prompt: Who did the job before Hamid?
Why does Hamid paint a crooked seven?
options: Because the door leans, so a crooked seven looks right on it.
questions.explanation: 'If I paint a straight seven on a leaning door, the seven looks wrong.'

questions.prompt: What did the city try to do?
options: Put printed metal number plates on the doors.
Teach Hamid to use a ruler.
questions.explanation: 'They printed numbers on metal plates... put the plates on a few doors.'

questions.prompt: What did the old woman say about the metal plates?
options: Her door looked like every other door, and she walked past her own home.
questions.explanation: 'Now my door looks like every other door. I walked past my own home.'

questions.prompt: What happened after the people complained?
What does Hamid know about the future?
options: One day his hand will shake too much and a machine will print the numbers.
questions.explanation: 'One day his hand will shake too much, and a machine will print the numbers.'

questions.prompt: What does 'every door wears a number that no other door has' suggest?
options: Each hand-painted number is unique, so people recognise their own home.
The doors are made of special wood.
questions.explanation: Uniqueness is the point — it is why people know their door.

options: A handmade imperfection can serve people better than a perfect, identical machine-made version.
Blue is the best colour for numbers.
questions.explanation: The crooked, unique number helps people more than the straight, identical plate.

cloze.template:
In the old part of Marrakech, the streets are {{b1}}. They turn left and right with no plan. The houses do not have street signs like in new cities. Instead, a man named Hamid paints the {{b2}} on the doors by hand. He has done this since he was a boy. His {{b3}} did it before him.

Hamid does not use a {{b4}}. He looks at the door, he dips his small brush in blue {{b5}}, and he writes the number. Sometimes the number is a little crooked. A tourist once said to him, 'Your seven is not {{b6}}.' Hamid laughed. He said, 'The door is old. It leans to the left. If I paint a straight seven on a leaning door, the seven looks wrong. A crooked seven on this door looks right.'

The city wanted to change this. They printed numbers on {{b7}} plates, all the same, all straight. They put the plates on a few doors. But the people did not like them. An old woman said, 'I knew my door by Hamid's blue four. Now my door looks like every other door. I walked past my own {{b8}}.'

So the city stopped. Hamid still walks the narrow streets with his {{b9}} and his small can of blue. He knows that one day his hand will shake too much, and a {{b10}} will print the numbers. But for now, every door in his quarter wears a number that no other door in the world has.

blanks.explanation: His father did it before him.
Dips his brush in blue paint.
I walked past my own home.
With his brush and his can of blue.
A machine will print the numbers.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Hamid argues a crooked number can be 'right' for a leaning door, while identical plates make people lose their own home. Where in your life is a small imperfection actually useful? When does 'all the same' make things worse, not better?
